project.name: jobsync
project.description: JobSync is a free, open-source, self-hosted web app for tracking and organizing job applications, with EURES/ESCO integration for European job discovery, AI resume review and job matching. Note: this project has over 100 source files; consider scoping analysis to a subdirectory for faster results.

tour.title: What JobSync Is
tour.description: Start with the README to understand the project's purpose: a free, self-hosted web app that tracks job applications and adds EURES/ESCO European job discovery plus AI resume review and matching. It outlines the Docker quick-start, the local-first AI integration (Ollama/DeepSeek), and the feature surface this tour will trace through real code. Read alongside CONTRIBUTING.md to see how the project expects changes to be made.

tour.title: How the App Boots
tour.description: Next.js runs two startup hooks before any request is served. The edge middleware sanitizes credential params from auth URLs, applies security headers, and runs the NextAuth handler; instrumentation.ts is the server-side register() hook that bootstraps the event consumers, the automation scheduler, health scheduler, CRM cron, retention cron, and the holiday pre-warm. Together they are the runtime entry point that wires every long-lived subsystem you will meet later in the tour.

tour.title: The Relational Model (Prisma Schema)
tour.description: Everything persists through Prisma over SQLite. The schema defines the entire relational model — jobs, automations, CRM persons, profile/resume, notifications, enrichment, and the new structured salary fields. The migration history records how that model evolved: the very first migration created the Job table, and the latest Welle 2 migration added structured salary columns. db.ts is the shared PrismaClient singleton that every server action queries through.

tour.title: Domain Models & Value Objects
tour.description: Above the database sits a hand-written domain layer. job.model.ts and person.model.ts (the CRM aggregate root) encode the ubiquitous language, state machines, and JSON value objects, while actionResult.ts defines the typed ActionResult<T> envelope that every server-action Repository returns. These are the most depended-upon domain files (actionResult and job.model rank high in fan-in), so understanding them unlocks the rest of the application layer.

tour.title: Server Actions as Repositories (DDD)
tour.description: Building on the domain models from Step 4, server actions in src/actions are the project's Repositories — one file per aggregate. job.actions.ts and profile.actions.ts have the highest fan-out in the codebase because they orchestrate persistence, ownership (IDOR) checks, events, and the ActionResult contract for the Job and Profile aggregates. This is where business rules meet the database.

tour.title: The Connector / Module ACL
tour.description: Every external integration goes through an Anti-Corruption Layer. manifest.ts defines the ModuleManifest each pluggable Module self-describes with; registry.ts is the single unified ModuleRegistry that stores them; and register-all.ts is the central side-effect importer that registers every module at boot. manifest.ts has very high fan-in — it is the shared kernel that the entire integration layer speaks through, translating foreign APIs into the DiscoveredVacancy domain type.

tour.title: A Connector in Action: Job Discovery
tour.description: Now see the ACL concretely. job-discovery/types.ts declares the DataSourceConnector interface and DiscoveredVacancy; the EURES module implements it for the European EURES API (location codes, ESCO occupation profiles, request language); and runner.ts orchestrates a full search — credential push, search, dedup, enrichment, matching, and save. The runner has the highest fan-out among lib files because it stitches the whole discovery pipeline together.

tour.title: Reference Data: The Currency Module (Welle 2)
tour.description: Reference-data modules are health-only Connectors that supply taxonomy/lookup data other features depend on. The currency module — added in Welle 2 — declares its manifest, types, and a static currency-data table, then implements ISO 4217 lookups behind the same registry pattern as discovery modules. It backs the structured salary feature you will meet in Step 9, showing how a new self-contained module slots in with no changes to the wizard or registry.

tour.title: A Pure Domain Library: Salary
tour.description: Welle 2 introduced a dedicated salary calculation library in src/lib/salary. parse-salary-range.ts turns free-text salary strings into structured values, build-job-salary.ts assembles the structured Job salary record (using the currency reference data from Step 8), and bonus.ts handles bonus/fixum math. These are pure, well-tested functions — a clean example of domain logic kept out of both the database and the UI.

tour.title: The Event Bus & Cross-Cutting Subsystems
tour.description: JobSync is event-driven. event-bus.ts is the TypedEventBus (a globalThis singleton surviving HMR) and event-schemas.ts holds the Zod schemas every consumer validates payloads against via safeParsePayload. The scheduler's RunCoordinator is the single mutex-guarded entry point for ALL automation runs, and the notification ChannelRouter fans a single notification out to in-app, webhook, email, and push channels. These were the subsystems bootstrapped back in Step 2.
tour.languageLesson: The globalThis singleton pattern (used by EventBus and RunCoordinator) survives Next.js Hot Module Replacement: the instance is stashed on globalThis so module re-evaluation in dev does not spawn duplicate schedulers or buses.

tour.title: The API Surface & GDPR-Safe AI Egress
tour.description: Two kinds of route handlers expose the app. with-api-auth.ts is the mandatory HOF wrapping every public /api/v1 route — CORS, API-key auth, IP and per-key rate limiting, error catch, and security headers. The AI resume routes send resume/job text to external LLMs, so they source PII redaction from the dependency-free src/lib/pii leaf to strip direct identifiers before any cloud transfer (GDPR Art. 5). This is the boundary between internal trust and the outside world.

tour.title: Internationalization Backbone
tour.description: Every user-visible string flows through the i18n adapter. index.ts is the client barrel (useTranslations, formatDate, formatNumber) and is the single highest fan-in file in the whole codebase — touched by nearly every component. server.ts is its server-only twin for Server Components, Actions, and route handlers. The adapter pattern means consumers never import internal dictionary modules directly.
tour.languageLesson: The i18n module is split into a client barrel (@/i18n) and a server barrel (@/i18n/server with `import "server-only"`). The server-only marker makes the bundler error if the file is ever pulled into a client component, preventing server secrets and Node APIs from leaking to the browser.

tour.title: The Presentation Layer
tour.description: Finally the UI, built on Shadcn primitives. The dashboard layout wires global providers (activity context, super-like celebrations, header, sidebar, toaster, undo listener) and renders the dashboard page. StagingContainer drives the Tinder-style vacancy review deck, and the automation WizardShell is a generic, manifest-driven rendering engine — it reads each module's connectorParamsSchema and widget overrides rather than hardcoding any module knowledge, so new connectors (from Steps 6-8) appear in the wizard for free.

tour.title: Specs, Containerization & CI
tour.description: Two pillars hold the project together at the edges. Allium specifications such as notification-dispatch and profile-resume are the authoritative source of truth for domain rules — code is checked against them, not the reverse. On the delivery side, the multi-stage Dockerfile compiles the app and ships a minimal non-root runtime image, docker-compose mounts the persistent data volume, and the GitHub Actions CI workflow installs dependencies and runs the test suite on every push.
tour.languageLesson: Multi-stage Docker builds use several FROM stages (base, deps, builder, runner): heavy build dependencies and the Prisma client generation live in the builder stage, while the final runner stage copies only the standalone output and runs as a non-root user — keeping the shipped image small and its attack surface minimal.

tour.title: How It's Tested
tour.description: JobSync ships nothing without tests. The Welle 2 salary work is a representative slice of the test pyramid: unit specs for the pure parsing and build functions, a schema spec for the add-job form validation, and a component spec for the salary input fields. Reading these closes the loop — you can see the Step 9 salary library and Step 13 UI exercised directly, and learn the project's Jest + Testing Library conventions for everything else.
